title: AGY-201: Standalone Operations (L200)
description: An in-depth, hands-on guide to mastering Standalone Operations using the antigravity visual desktop board. Designed specifically for visual learners, solution architects, and technical business analysts, this course covers the configuration and management of asynchronous agent workflows, advanced prompt engineering techniques for structured document generation, and deep-dive troubleshooting of web-scraping agents via visual playback tapes. By the end of this course, you will be able to orchestrate complex multi-agent scraping pipelines, debug live selector issues, and compile clean markdown analytical dashboards with zero placeholders.

highlights.title: Asynchronous Coordination
highlights.description: Master the visual management of multiple parallel agent cards, tracking progress, state transitions, and intermediate human-in-the-loop approvals.

highlights.title: Zero-Placeholder Prompting
highlights.description: Learn the specific prompt design rules and templates needed to coerce agentic workflows into producing publication-grade markdown documentation.

highlights.title: Visual Browser Playback
highlights.description: Acquire the skills to record, analyze, and step through browser automation playback tapes to resolve dynamic page loading and shifting DOM elements.

highlights.title: Specialist Badge Readiness
highlights.description: Prepare for the AGY Certified Operations Specialist Capstone by building a three-source competitor monitor.

blocks.content: Welcome to **AGY-201: Standalone Operations**. In the Google Antigravity (AGY) ecosystem, the standalone desktop application, known as **antigravity**, serves as a visual "Mission Control" for managing autonomous, long-running agent tasks. While command-line terminals (`agy`) or side-by-side IDE environments (`antigravity-ide`) are preferred by developer personas, business analysts, solutions architects, and operations specialists require a dashboard that offers high-level visibility, parallel tracking, and browser-level action playbacks.

The `antigravity` visual dashboard decouples the execution daemon from the client interface. This ensures that you can trigger, monitor, and guide asynchronous agent actions on a local workstation or remote virtual machine without losing real-time feedback.
The Four Interface Panes
The standalone application is structured into four distinct functional sections:

items: **The Tasks Kanban Board:** Serves as the central visual repository for all active, proposed, and completed agent tasks. Each task is represented as a card detailing the agent's goal and execution progress.
**The Execution Console:** Displays raw logs, terminal shell inputs/outputs, model request metrics, and security authorization prompts.

**The Playback Viewport:** Renders the live graphical display of browser sessions. When an agent launches a browser automation (e.g. via Playwright), you can visually watch the virtual page scroll, click, and render components.
**The Output Panel:** Displays formatted markdown documents, reports, code snippets, or verification status generated by the agent loops.

blocks.content: Every session in `antigravity` runs within a designated workspace directory. This workspace boundary serves as a filesystem security wall. The agent is locked into this path and cannot traverse or write to folders outside of it unless explicitly whitelisted in local configuration files.
Launching the Standalone Interface
To launch the visual dashboard, you must prepare a workspace directory and execute the startup command, passing the workspace path as a parameter:

# Create a dedicated project directory
mkdir -p ~/agy-workspaces/competitive-tracker
cd ~/agy-workspaces/competitive-tracker

# Initialize the directory for AGY operations
agy init --workspace-path .

# Launch the visual dashboard interface on a custom port
antigravity --workspace-path ~/agy-workspaces/competitive-tracker --port 8080
Workspace Directory Structure
Upon running `agy init`, the directory is initialized with a hidden control folder `.gemini` and a workspace rule file `.gemini.md`. The default layout will look like this:

competitive-tracker/
├── .gemini/
│   ├── config.json       # Visual board configurations and model endpoints
│   ├── permissions.json  # Whitelisted/blocked commands and approval gates
│   └── state/            # Temporary logs and task card states
├── .gemini.md            # Markdown file defining custom workspace rules
├── data/                 # Directory reserved for scraped pricing tables
└── reports/              # Folder for generated analysis documents
Check Your Understanding
**Question 1:** Which pane of the `antigravity` interface displays the live visual actions of the agent as it navigates websites?

items: A) The Tasks Kanban Board
B) The Playback Viewport
C) The Execution Console
D) The Output Panel

blocks.content:
**Correct Answer: B**

**Immediate Feedback:** The Playback Viewport mirrors the headful browser window driven by Playwright, allowing you to watch the agent interact with web pages in real-time.
**Question 2:** What is the primary purpose of running `agy init` prior to starting the `antigravity` application?

items: A) To download dependencies like Chromium and Playwright
B) To compile and package the frontend React codebase
C) To establish the local control files, permissions, and rules that restrict the agent's scope
D) To sign in to the Google Cloud Console

blocks.content:
**Correct Answer: C**

**Immediate Feedback:** Sourcing `agy init` sets up the necessary `.gemini/` configuration files, establishing the security permissions and boundary controls for the agent.

In traditional development setups, you run a command in your shell and wait for it to complete. In standalone operations, tasks are often complex and long-running. The `antigravity` application handles this using **Agent Cards** managed asynchronously on a Kanban board.

When a new task is created, a dedicated agent card is spawned. This card runs as a separate thread, letting you dispatch multiple agents in parallel to perform independent missions (e.g. scraping different websites or compiling different reports).
Agent Card Lifecycle States
Every task card on the board moves through a defined set of execution states:

items: **Proposed:** The task description is analyzed, and the agent is preparing to outline its strategy.
**Planning:** The agent drafts a step-by-step implementation plan and displays it for review.
**Executing:** The agent is active, performing file system operations, executing whitelisted console commands, or scraping pages.
**Awaiting Approval:** The agent reaches a security gate or user-defined checkpoint and pauses for manual authorization.
**Completed:** The task succeeded, and the final output (typically formatted markdown) is loaded into the Output Panel.
**Failed:** The agent encountered a fatal error (e.g. security block or execution timeout) and aborted.

blocks.content: Never launch `antigravity` with the `--auto-approve` flag on a native host (Tier 1 or Tier 2). This bypasses the approval loop entirely, giving the agent full control to execute commands and alter files on your primary workstation without confirmation. Auto-approval should only be active inside containerized sandboxes like `agy-box`.
Steering the Collaboration Loop
The Human-in-the-Loop pattern is established via three core interactive decisions on the card when it enters the **Awaiting Approval** state:

items: **Approve:** Authorizes the proposed step or command to proceed.
**Redirect:** Pauses the execution and lets the user type manual directions (e.g. instructing the agent to fix a selector or search a different folder).
**Reject:** Denies the action and immediately kills the agent card thread.

blocks.content: Configuring Permissions and Rules
You can declare execution limits by editing `.gemini/permissions.json` directly. This maps command patterns to automated approvals, blocks, or manual confirmations:
Check Your Understanding
**Question 1:** What action should you take when an agent card enters the 'Awaiting Approval' state but you notice its proposed shell command is incorrect?

items: A) Click Approve and let it fail naturally
B) Press the workspace Panic Button
C) Click Redirect, provide specific instructions to modify the command, and resume
D) Manually run the command in a separate terminal window

blocks.content:
**Correct Answer: C**

**Immediate Feedback:** Selecting Redirect pauses the agent, opens an input box for custom steering instructions, and prevents the agent from running incorrect or destructive commands.
**Question 2:** Which file in the `.gemini/` control folder defines which command prefixes require human-in-the-loop approvals?

items: A) config.json
B) state.json
C) permissions.json
D) rules.md

blocks.content:
**Correct Answer: C**

**Immediate Feedback:** The `permissions.json` file contains parameters for `allowed_commands`, `blocked_commands`, and `require_approval`, defining the exact authorization boundaries.

AI agents are excellent at compiling summaries, writing SOWs, and organizing structured analyses. However, left unguided, they frequently resort to placeholders (e.g. `[Insert Date Here]`, `TODO: Add feature list`, or `Lorem Ipsum`). For business analysts and architects, these placeholders represent compilation failures.

To prevent this, operations specialists must master the **Zero-Placeholder Rule**. This design rule states that all generated output documents must be complete, production-grade text. If a piece of data is missing from the source files, the agent must estimate it, mark it explicitly as 'Data Unavailable' based on the sources, or calculate a logical approximation rather than writing bracketed placeholders.
The Four Pillars of a Production Prompt
When writing instructions to generate documents inside the `antigravity` dashboard, structure your prompt into four key areas:

items: **Persona & Role:** Establishes the professional background and standards of the agent.
**Context & Inputs:** Feeds the agent the exact files or data sources to parse.
**Strict Constraints:** Sets the rules, including the Zero-Placeholder Rule and the target format.
**Structure & Outline:** Declares the exact markdown headings and sections expected in the final document.

blocks.content: Standard Competitor Pricing Summary Prompt
The following is a production prompt template designed for compiling competitor pricing matrices. It can be copied directly into the prompt bar of the visual board:

Role: Senior Competitive Intelligence Analyst
Task: Generate a Competitive Pricing Summary from the scraped data files in './data/'.

Input Sources:
- File: './data/competitor_a.json'
- File: './data/competitor_b.json'
- File: './data/competitor_c.json'

Constraints:
1. ZERO-PLACEHOLDER RULE: Under no circumstances will you write placeholders, empty brackets, or TODO items. Every sentence must contain fully written text. If a pricing feature is missing, write 'Data Not Provided in Sources'.
2. Output format must be GitHub Flavored Markdown (GFM).
3. You must include a structured comparison table with columns: Competitor, Plan Name, Monthly Cost, Key Features, and Data Source.
4. Write a 3-paragraph executive summary detailing pricing gaps.

Structure:
# Competitive Pricing Analysis
## Executive Summary
[Your 3 paragraphs detailing pricing differentials]
## Comparison Matrix
[Your GFM comparison table]
Standard GFM tables are rendered natively by the `antigravity` Output Panel, displaying clean, visually styled interactive spreadsheets directly on the board.
Check Your Understanding
**Question 1:** According to the Zero-Placeholder Rule, what should the agent write if a competitor's monthly subscription cost is missing from the scraped files?

items: A) Leaving the cost field empty or using brackets like `[Cost]`
B) Writing a clear fallback statement like 'Data Not Provided in Sources' or providing a logical approximation based on other tiers
C) Halting the execution plan and throwing a terminal exception
D) Leaving a TODO comment in the output file

blocks.content:
**Correct Answer: B**

**Immediate Feedback:** The Zero-Placeholder Rule prohibits the use of bracketed values or TODOs. The agent must write explicit fallback notes or compute estimated values based on context.
**Question 2:** Why is it recommended to specify the exact outline structure in a document generation prompt?

items: A) It decreases the agent's API token consumption
B) It allows the Output Panel to parse and display the sections with the correct visual layout and headers
C) It forces the agent to bypass execution approval checks
D) It converts the document automatically to a PDF

blocks.content:
**Correct Answer: B**

**Immediate Feedback:** Declaring a concrete markdown outline ensures that the agent's output is structured correctly for visual rendering on the dashboard.

When scraping data from modern websites, agents often face obstacles like single-page applications (React, Angular) that load contents dynamically, or firewalls that block programmatic access. To troubleshoot these issues, `antigravity` uses a visual browser execution model.

When an agent starts a web scraping task, it runs a virtual browser in **Headful Mode** (using Playwright). This mirrors the agent's actions directly inside the **Playback Viewport**. Furthermore, the dashboard records these actions as **Visual Playback Tapes** (saved as `.webm` video recordings and step-by-step screenshots) under the `.gemini/state/tapes/` workspace directory.
Isolating Failures using the Playback Viewport

If a scraping run fails due to a locator timeout or dynamic content shift, you can open the playback tape in the dashboard. Using the control panel (Play, Pause, Step Forward, Step Backward), you can inspect the agent's actions second-by-second. This lets you see precisely why it failed (e.g. a loading spinner was still active, or a modal pop-up blocked the button click).
Troubleshooting screenshots are automatically captured and saved under `.gemini/state/tapes/` whenever a scraper command exits with a non-zero code or times out on a DOM selector.
Standard Playwright Scraper Script
Below is the robust Python scraper configuration executed by the agent. It sets custom viewports, overrides the user agent, and waits for network idle states to ensure dynamic elements load:

import asyncio
from playwright.async_api import async_playwright

async def run_scraper(target_url, output_path):
    async with async_playwright() as p:
        # Launch headful browser to integrate with the Playback Viewport
        browser = await p.chromium.launch(headless=False)
        context = await browser.new_context(
            viewport={'width': 1280, 'height': 800},
            user_agent='Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 10.0; Win64; x64) AppleWebKit/537.36'
        )
        page = await context.new_page()
        
        print(f"Navigating to {target_url}...")
        await page.goto(target_url, wait_until='networkidle')
        
        # Wait for the target table to render dynamically
        try:
            await page.wait_for_selector('table.pricing-matrix', timeout=5000)
            table_html = await page.locator('table.pricing-matrix').inner_html()
            with open(output_path, 'w') as f:
                f.write(table_html)
            print(f"Successfully saved data to {output_path}")
        except Exception as e:
            print(f"Selector timeout occurred: {e}")
            # Capture screenshot for visual tape troubleshooting
            await page.screenshot(path='./data/error_screenshot.png')
            raise e
        finally:
            await browser.close()

# Execute scraper
asyncio.run(run_scraper('https://mock-competitor.com/pricing', './data/scraped_table.html'))
Fallback Strategies for Scraper Prompts
When writing scraping commands, include these standard fallbacks in your agent instructions:

items: **Text-Based Matching:** If the class selector (e.g. `table.pricing-matrix`) is not found, attempt to locate containers using text headers (e.g. searching for 'Pricing' or 'Subscription Plans').
**State Hydration Wait:** If the scraped container returns empty, instruct the agent to pause for 3 seconds to let dynamic content hydrate before retrying.
**Error Capture:** Direct the agent to save a diagnostic screenshot to the `.gemini/state/tapes/` folder whenever a selector fails.

blocks.content: Check Your Understanding
**Question 1:** Why does the AGY browser execute in headful mode (headless=False) during visual scraping operations?

items: A) To decrease CPU and memory consumption during executions
B) To allow the browser window to render inside the Playback Viewport so the user can inspect the live interaction
C) To bypass basic local network proxy setups
D) Because Chromium cannot run headlessly on Linux servers

blocks.content:
**Correct Answer: B**

**Immediate Feedback:** Running in headful mode is required to project the browser GUI into the Playback Viewport, enabling visual tracking and recording of the agent's actions.
**Question 2:** Where does the agent save troubleshooting screenshots when a Playwright selector timeout occurs?

items: A) Inside the host's `Downloads` folder
B) In the remote cloud database
C) Under the `.gemini/state/tapes/` folder in the active workspace
D) In the project's node_modules directory

blocks.content:
**Correct Answer: C**

**Immediate Feedback:** Diagnostics, screenshots, and visual recordings are centralized inside the `.gemini/state/tapes/` folder for easy inspection.

The capstone project is the final validation challenge for the L200 Standalone Operations track. Successful completion of this project is required to earn the **AGY Certified Operations Specialist** credential.
Project Scenario & Scope

You are a Competitive Intelligence Analyst at Tridorian. You are tasked with analyzing the pricing models of three competitor services. You must orchestrate three parallel agent cards on the `antigravity` dashboard to monitor three target sites, scrape their pricing details using visual browser automation, troubleshoot any loading delays, and compile a consolidated pricing matrix following the Zero-Placeholder Rule.
Step-by-Step Implementation Blueprint
**Step 1: Workspace and Target Configuration**
Prepare your local directory and create a configuration mapping the simulated competitor URLs:

# Create folders
mkdir -p ~/agy-workspaces/competitive-tracker/data
cd ~/agy-workspaces/competitive-tracker

# Create the competitors list mapping file
cat << 'EOF' > competitors.json
[
  {"id": "competitor_a", "url": "http://localhost:3000/comp-a"},
  {"id": "competitor_b", "url": "http://localhost:3000/comp-b"},
  {"id": "competitor_c", "url": "http://localhost:3000/comp-c"}
]
EOF
**Step 2: Dispatch the Aggregation Prompt**
In the `antigravity` dashboard prompt input field, enter the following orchestrator instructions:

Task: Parallel Competitive Pricing Aggregator

Instructions:
1. Read the list of target websites from './competitors.json'.
2. For each competitor, spawn a web scraping subagent.
3. Navigate to each URL using the browser automation tools.
4. Extract the pricing plans. On Competitor C's site, note that the pricing table loads dynamically after a 2-second delay. Ensure you wait for the selector 'div.pricing-card' to be visible.
5. Save the scraped data for each source in JSON format under './data/'.
6. Consolidate the findings into a single report in the root directory named 'pricing_matrix.md'.
7. The report must contain a 3-paragraph summary and a GFM table comparing plans and pricing. Follow the Zero-Placeholder Rule.
**Step 3: Monitor Execution & Debug Competitor C**
During execution, the agent card for Competitor C will pause and report a selector timeout because of a dynamic loader spinner. You must resolve this using active steering:

items: Open the Playback Viewport for the Competitor C scraper card.
Examine the visual recording tape under `.gemini/state/tapes/` to confirm that the spinner was active and verify the element class name.
Click Redirect on the card, and input the following corrective instructions: 'Modify the script to wait for networkidle state and add a 3000ms delay before attempting to select the pricing cards.'
Click Resume to let the agent compile the data successfully.

blocks.content: **Step 4: Automated Verification**
Once the consolidated `pricing_matrix.md` has been compiled in your workspace, execute the local validation checks in your terminal to assert project integrity:

# Run the validation command against the generated output
agy verify --course agy-201 --target ./pricing_matrix.md
Check Your Understanding
**Question 1:** Which CLI command is used to locally check your workspace deliverables against the certification criteria for course `agy-201`?

items: A) `antigravity run`
B) `agy verify --course agy-201 --target ./pricing_matrix.md`
C) `node scripts/sync-docs.js`
D) `playwright test`

blocks.content:
**Correct Answer: B**

**Immediate Feedback:** Running `agy verify` with the specific course code and target file runs local assertions against your capstone output files to verify completion.
**Question 2:** What steering feedback corrects the agent when it times out trying to scrape a dynamic client-rendered page?

items: A) Reject the step and run a basic curl command instead
B) Redirect the agent to wait for the network idle state and add a specific wait timeout in milliseconds
C) Restart the visual board daemon on a different port
D) Add the target site domain to the blocked list in the permissions file

blocks.content:
**Correct Answer: B**

**Immediate Feedback:** Instructing the agent to wait for network idle states and introducing a wait timeout tells Playwright to let client-side JS finish rendering before querying elements.
